name: context-os
description: Stop Claude burning your tokens re-exploring your own code every session. context-os maps your repo into small, portable context files — one per folder plus a root index — that Claude (or Codex, or Gemini) reads on demand instead of re-scanning your whole project. It maps what matters: it ranks folders and deeply maps only the strategic ones (the code hubs and entry points), keeps a cheap skeleton for the rest, and folds pure docs/data folders into their parent — instead of blindly mapping every folder. Or go fully lazy: map the whole repo as free skeletons once, then /context-os-catchup enriches only the folders you actually worked in this session, so enrichment cost tracks real use. It keeps the maps honest: a drift check flags a map the moment its folder's code changes, so a stale map warns you instead of quietly lying. And /snapshot captures your current session — a compacted summary plus the work state — into one portable file, so you can stop mid-task, move to another machine or another model, and pick up cold. Five commands (/context-os, /context-os-catchup, /context-os-update, /context-os-status, /snapshot), a folder ranker, sharded per-folder enrichment agents with a self-healing repair loop, a drift-detection hook, and two honest, separate numbers: a token-save ceiling measured against your real source, and a per-session measurement of what map use actually delivered (a hook logs whether the agent read the map or re-read the source, so you measure the saving instead of guessing it). It also lets an agent retrieve the exact original behind any map reference (compress-cache-retrieve), exposes the maps + retrieval over an MCP server so any agent or proxy can consume them, and now maps config/docs/data folders too — not just code. It never invents a node — every node traces to real code — and never touches your existing CLAUDE.md beyond its own marked block. Free, offline, $0, no server, no API keys, stdlib-only.
